core_concept: Three Branches of US Government: Legislative, Executive, and Judicial
facts: The legislative branch, called Congress, is the only branch that can make laws; it is divided into the Senate and the House of Representatives.
The executive branch, led by the President, carries out and enforces the laws that Congress makes, not the laws the President makes.
The judicial branch, including the Supreme Court, interprets laws to decide what they mean and whether they have been followed correctly, not to enforce them.
The President can check the legislative branch by vetoing a law, which means the President says no to a bill passed by Congress, showing that no branch has all the power.
misconceptions: The President makes the laws for the country.
The Supreme Court is in charge of enforcing laws and punishing people who break them.
